stories.headline: Man fatally shot in Denver's Green Valley Ranch neighborhood; no arrests made
stories.summary:
A man was shot and killed Friday evening in Denver's Green Valley Ranch neighborhood, according to Denver police. Officers responded around 5 p.m. to the area of 48th Avenue and Argonne Street after reports of a shooting.

The victim was transported to a hospital, where he was pronounced dead. No arrests had been made as of Friday evening, and police released no information about the identity of the victim or a possible motive.

The investigation remains ongoing. Denver police are asking anyone with information to contact Metro Denver Crime Stoppers.

stories.headline: Las Vegas man sentenced to prison for stealing $65,000 from Coinstar machines along I-70 corridor
stories.summary:
A Las Vegas man will serve up to four years in a Colorado prison after pleading guilty to stealing more than $65,000 in coins from Coinstar kiosks at grocery stores along the Interstate 70 corridor between Grand Junction and metro Denver. Richard Pena, 37, broke into 11 Coinstar machines over four days in November 2021, exploiting the fact that all Coinstar kiosks in the United States are keyed identically upon manufacture.

Pena was seen on surveillance footage entering stores with a hand dolly and removing large bins of coins, which he carted out to a waiting van. Colorado's statewide grand jury indicted him on 11 counts of third-degree burglary, but he ultimately pleaded guilty to one count of class-4 felony theft covering all 11 incidents.

A Denver District Court judge ordered Pena to pay $77,044.06 in restitution to Coinstar for stolen money and damage to machines. Pena is already serving a federal sentence in Oregon for similar thefts in other states. Colorado Attorney General Phil Weiser said the case involved losses at stores in Denver, Adams, Jefferson, Eagle and Mesa counties.

stories.headline: Cargill lockout enters 83rd day, threatening economic survival of Fort Morgan
stories.summary:
The Cargill meatpacking plant in Fort Morgan has remained largely shut for 83 days after the company locked out its unionized workforce in May amid a contentious contract dispute with Teamsters Local 455. The facility, which employs more than 2,000 workers and accounts for more than 20% of the town's population, is the economic engine of the rural northeastern Colorado city of about 11,000 residents.

Teamsters Local 455 accuses Cargill of refusing to offer meaningful wage increases despite the company reporting substantial profits. Cargill, the largest privately held corporation in America by revenue at $154 billion annually, says its proposal offers substantial benefits and that the lockout was not a decision made lightly.

Fort Morgan officials have already implemented budget cuts and frozen hiring in anticipation of steeper revenue declines. City Manager Brent Nation described the situation as potentially a "generational event" for the community, where the average resident earns just $26,000 a year and more than half the population identifies as Hispanic. Many of the locked-out workers are immigrants or refugees.

stories.headline: Frontier Airlines to add Starlink Wi-Fi service, launch new Denver-Fort Lauderdale nonstop route
stories.summary:
Denver-based Frontier Airlines announced it will deploy Starlink satellite Wi-Fi service on its aircraft starting next year, partnering with SpaceX's subsidiary through an arrangement brokered by private equity firm Indigo Partners. The deal will equip more than 1,000 jets across Frontier and several international partner airlines with a system managed directly by SpaceX terminal engineering teams, bypassing traditional third-party connectivity vendors.

Frontier CEO Jimmy Dempsey said the partnership will give customers the ability to work, stream and browse throughout their flights. Unlike legacy systems that must disconnect below 10,000 feet, the Starlink setup will use an electronically steered antenna to maintain a continuous connection from gate to gate, benefiting both passengers and airline operations such as diagnostics and baggage tracking.

In a separate announcement, Frontier said it will launch a new daily nonstop flight between Denver International Airport and Fort Lauderdale, Florida, beginning November 20, bringing its total Denver destinations to 55. Introductory fares for the new route are set at $99. The airline has not disclosed what the Wi-Fi service will cost passengers.

stories.headline: Cannabist Co. closing Denver grow facility, cutting 50 jobs as cannabis wholesale prices collapse
stories.summary:
The Cannabist Co. has notified the Colorado Department of Labor and Employment that it will permanently close its north Denver cannabis cultivation and manufacturing facility at 4750 Nome St., eliminating 50 jobs beginning September 11. The company filed a required Worker Adjustment and Retraining Notification letter and said affected employees will continue receiving base pay and benefits for 60 days.

The closure reflects broader distress in Colorado's cannabis cultivation industry, where wholesale marijuana prices have plummeted from a pandemic-era peak of roughly $1,700 per pound to less than $600 per pound. The number of active recreational cultivation licenses in Colorado has fallen by nearly half since the market's 2021 peak, according to the state Marijuana Enforcement Division.

The Cannabist Co. filed for bankruptcy protection in Delaware on March 25 after defaulting on a loan, and has been liquidating assets across multiple states to satisfy creditors. The closure follows similar moves by other operators: PharmaCann shuttered a facility near the National Western Complex in March, and Eaze closed a 92,000-square-foot Denver facility in June 2025.

stories.headline: Colorado homeowners insurance costs top nation; new state law offers grants for fortified roofs
stories.summary:
Colorado ranks first in the nation for the largest cumulative increase in home insurance premiums since 2020, with the average homeowner now paying an estimated $4,164 annually in 2026, according to insurance comparison site Insurify. Some residents have seen their premiums quintuple over the past decade, driven primarily by hail damage claims, which represent the top insurance payout category in the state.

A new state law establishes the Strengthen Colorado Homes Enterprise, which will provide grants to help homeowners upgrade to fortified roofs designed to better withstand hail. The program is funded by a 0.5% fee on insurance companies operating in Colorado, with $30.2 million set aside in the state budget. At least 85% of those funds must go directly to grants, with individual awards expected to range from $7,500 to $10,000.

Colorado Insurance Commissioner Michael Conway said the grants are intended to cover the additional cost of upgrading to a fortified roof — estimated at $3,000 to $5,000 for a 2,000-square-foot home — rather than the full replacement cost. A seven-member board is being formed to administer the program; applications are currently open through the state insurance division.

stories.headline: Colorado wildfire tracker: Aspen Acres fire 61% contained; Gold Mountain and Ferris fires also burning
stories.summary:
Multiple large wildfires continue to burn across Colorado, with the Aspen Acres fire in Custer and Pueblo counties now 61% contained after scorching 100,224 acres. More than 775 structures have been destroyed and mandatory evacuations remain in effect for communities near the Pueblo-Custer county border, including Rye and San Isabel. More than 1,700 personnel were working the fire as of July 17.

The Gold Mountain fire near Ouray has grown to 37,809 acres and is only 13% contained. A helicopter pilot, Nicholas Dale, 56, of Canada, was killed July 12 when his aircraft crashed into Silver Jack Reservoir while fighting the blaze. Gov. Jared Polis has declared a disaster emergency for the Gold Mountain fire to allow additional state agencies to assist local response teams.

The Ferris fire in San Juan National Forest in Montezuma and Dolores counties has reached 64,881 acres and is 83% contained. A fourth fire, the Willow fire near Leadville, has burned 7,103 acres and is 37% contained after starting near Turquoise Lake.

stories.headline: Civil lawsuit blames San Isabel Electric for sparking Aspen Acres fire in southern Colorado
stories.summary:
A civil lawsuit has been filed against San Isabel Electric Association alleging the rural electric cooperative is responsible for igniting the Aspen Acres fire, which has burned more than 100,000 acres in Custer and Pueblo counties and destroyed more than 775 structures. The fire, which started June 29, forced evacuations of roughly 3,800 homes affecting more than 11,000 people.

The lawsuit marks an early legal action in what could become extensive litigation stemming from one of Colorado's most destructive wildfires in recent memory. Investigators have classified the fire as human-caused, though the specific cause remains under investigation.

San Isabel Electric serves customers across a large swath of southern Colorado. Utility companies have faced increasing scrutiny and legal liability across the West as drought conditions and aging infrastructure combine to raise wildfire risk.

stories.headline: Water World opens Summit Canyon, its largest expansion since the 1980s, in Federal Heights
stories.summary:
Water World in Federal Heights unveiled Summit Canyon this week, a 2.5-acre expansion that represents the largest renovation at the park since the 1980s. The centerpiece is a four-tower slide complex anchored by a 60-foot Lookout Tower, the highest point in the park, featuring four distinct slides including Aspen Falls, which uses a trap door drop capsule, and Columbine Cascade, with more than 500 feet of twists through translucent fiberglass.

The expansion also includes Critter Cove, a 7,500-square-foot children's play area with five nature-themed slides and spray features, and Emerald Lake, an adults-only leisure pool with shade, floats and in-water seating. One of the new slides, Marmot Mayhem, features Colorado's first HIVE waterslide technology, which uses tight spiraling helixes to generate G-forces.

Water World director of enterprise operations Bob Owens said the goal was to create attractions for guests of all ages and thrill levels under one area. The park is located at 8801 Pecos St. in Federal Heights.

stories.headline: Rockies split weekend series with Reds; Sugano strong in return from injured list
stories.summary:
The Colorado Rockies split a three-game series with the Cincinnati Reds at Coors Field, with TJ Rumfield delivering a career-best four-hit game including a 456-foot home run in a 10-3 Colorado victory Saturday. Tomoyuki Sugano returned from the 15-day injured list to throw 6 1/3 innings, allowing three runs while striking out three and walking none, improving his record to 9-4 on the season.

The Reds won Friday's game 7-2 behind Brady Singer, who allowed four hits in seven innings, and a two-homer performance from Spencer Steer including an inside-the-park home run. Cincinnati also won the series opener, with the Athletics beating Colorado in a separate road game 7-5.

At 40-60 heading into Sunday's finale, the Rockies are three wins from matching their total win output from all of last season. Colorado needs to go 23-39 the rest of the way to avoid a fourth consecutive 100-loss season.

stories.headline: Nuggets feel confident about retaining restricted free agent Peyton Watson amid outside interest
stories.summary:
The Denver Nuggets are confident in their ability to retain restricted free agent Peyton Watson despite growing interest from the Milwaukee Bucks, Los Angeles Clippers and Atlanta Hawks, according to reports. As a restricted free agent, the Nuggets retain the right to match any offer sheet Watson signs with another team.

Former Nuggets teammate Michael Porter Jr., now with the Brooklyn Nets, has been publicly attempting to recruit Watson to Brooklyn. Porter made the pitch on a Twitch stream, saying Watson was "about to sign for $130 million" and that he was trying to bring him to Brooklyn.

Watson, a 23-year-old forward, has developed into a key defensive piece for Denver since being drafted 22nd overall in 2022. His retention is considered important to the Nuggets' roster continuity heading into the 2026-27 season.

stories.headline: Broncos training camp preview: Defensive line faces questions after Franklin-Myers' departure
stories.summary:
The Denver Broncos head into training camp — with rookies reporting Wednesday — facing a significant void on the defensive line after John Franklin-Myers signed a three-year, $63 million deal with the Tennessee Titans in free agency. Franklin-Myers recorded 65 tackles and 14.5 sacks across his two seasons in Denver, making him one of the team's most productive pass rushers.

The Broncos chose not to re-sign Franklin-Myers after extending Jonathon Cooper, Matt Roach and D.J. Jones on multi-year deals. Roach, who recorded 6.5 sacks as a backup over two seasons, is a candidate to shift from defensive tackle to defensive end. Sai'vion Jones and third-round draft pick Chidozie Onyedim from Texas A&M are also expected to compete for expanded roles.

The Denver Gazette's position group analysis concludes the defensive line remains a strength of the roster despite the loss, noting that Onyedim posted 48 tackles, 8.5 tackles for loss and 2.5 sacks during a standout senior season. Coaches are expected to be patient with Onyedim's development while multiple veterans compete to fill Franklin-Myers' production.

weather.notable: Nearly 9 degrees above the average high for this time of year.

joke.joke: I love the outdoors. I just prefer to experience them from behind a window.
